data.title: Oracle: AI Juggernaut Starts Delivering On Its Promises
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The last time I wrote about Oracle Corporation (NYSE: NYSE:ORCL), back in March 2025, I analyzed the company’s third quarter earnings report and investigated whether there had been any significant developments regarding the Stargate initiative. I also examined the existing demand for OCI to see if there are any cracks. I had a Buy rating on the stock.

Since my article was published, the stock has surged, gaining 35%, comfortably outperforming the S&P 500 (SP500), which gained nearly 8% during the same period.

In this article, I dissect the company’s Q4 report and explore the key takeaways, especially with respect to the demand for OCI as well as how the company is dealing with the tariff-riddled macroenvironment that it currently operates in.

A Snapshot of Oracle’s Q4 Results

ORCL bucked its recent trend by beating on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. More specifically, Q4 revenues came in at $15.9 billion, up 11.3% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $310 million. Non-GAAP EPS of $1.70 beat analyst estimates by $0.06. The current quarter also saw revenue growth nearly doubling compared to the previous quarter. Cloud infrastructure delivered yet another quarter of remarkable growth, jumping 52% y/y. Non-GAAP operating margins came in at 44%, a decline of 300 bps y/y but flat on a sequential basis.

Over the last twelve months, the company generated $20.82 billion in operating cash flows. As a result of a surge in capital expenditures, which came in at $21.2 billion in Q4, free cash flows, however, turned negative. Finally, management maintained their quarterly dividend at $0.50 per share. For the full year, ORCL generated revenues of $57.4 billion, up 8% y/y, and non-GAAP diluted EPS of $6.03, which translates to a y/y growth of 8.4%.

With respect to first quarter guidance, management now sees total revenues growing between 11% and 13% y/y in constant currency, with total cloud revenues expected to register a y/y growth in the range between 26% and 30%. Non-GAAP EPS is expected to grow between 5% to 7% and come in the range of $1.46 and $1.50, assuming a tax rate of 19%. Management also projected FY26 revenue growth to double and come in at $67 billion, on the back of a projected RPO growth of more than 100% y/y.

Q4 Shows Company is Starting to Deliver on Its Promises & There’s More to Come

There has been a lot of debate on the amount of spending that the AI players have undertaken, especially with many of them yet to fully translate the spending into meaningful growth. When it comes to ORCL however, there should be no such debate in my opinion. This, to me, was the main takeaway from the company’s Q4 report. In other words, management not only promised investors that their spending would translate into growth but also delivered on the promise.

One of the metrics that offer evidence on this is the Remaining Performance Obligations (RPO). This metric has seen a remarkable growth in recent quarters. Q4 was no different as the metric stood at $138 billion, which translates to a y/y growth of 41%. That’s an increase of $8 billion from the previous quarter and is the second straight quarter when this metric has crossed $100 billion. 33% of the RPOs are projected to be recognized as revenue in the next 12 months, which translates to $45.5 billion.

Despite all these blowout numbers, investors can still point to the fact that the RPO growth has dramatically declined from 63% y/y in Q3 to 41% y/y in Q4. This deceleration is the evidence that the management has delivered on its promise of translating their investments into meaningful growth.

As seen from the above figure, the decline in RPO growth can be attributed to more capacity coming online, which enabled the company to convert RPOs into revenues. The company’s investments have paid off to such an extent that the it's revenues hit double-digits for the first time in five quarters.

Moreover, despite the company’s investments translating into actual growth, based on management commentary, demand continues to outstrip supply. As such, the company is not slowing down its spending. After spending $21.2 billion in capital expenditures in FY24, the company now projects FY25 Capex to be in excess of $25 billion. Management, in my opinion, has every right to boost its spending. This is because not only is revenue growth expected to nearly double from 8.4% in FY25 to 16% in FY26 but RPO, which is the ideal indicator of demand, is expected to grow more than 100% in FY26. In absolute terms, this would translate to more than $275 billion. Given that RPOs are noncancelable contracts, this offers further evidence as to why ORCL needs to keep spending. At the same time, based on the revenue growth trajectory, investors can take solace in the fact that the company’s investments are actually translating into growth. Not many AI players can offer evidence as concrete as ORCL.

Moreover, management commentary also suggested that Stargate’s formation is still not complete, and as such, the massive FY26 RPO number doesn’t involve any meaningful contribution from Stargate. In my last article on ORCL, I pointed out that any growth via Stargate is a bonus for ORCL. The fact that this remains the case even in Q4 is promising in my opinion.

Lastly, investors should also have no concerns about any delays in ORCL's capacity coming online. During the earnings call, management categorically stated that they have no issues in obtaining the GPUs, which should offer investors more confidence in the management’s revenue projections for both FY26 and even FY27.

Taking all these together, Q4 proved that ORCL continues to be an AI juggernaut. And with the company’s other segments also firing on all cylinders, except for Services, investors, in my opinion would be hard-pressed to find any meaningful weakness in its AI journey.

Valuation

Forward P/E Approach

Price Target

$206.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

32.1x

PV of Projected FY26 EPS

$6.45

Source: Company’s Q4FY25 Earnings Call Transcript, LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), and Author’s Calculations.

ORCL, according to LSEG Data, currently trades at forward P/E of 25.9x, which makes it more expensive compared to Salesforce (CRM) and IBM (IBM) but cheaper than Workday (WDAY) and SAP (SAP). Relative to its historical levels, the company is more expensive, given that it’s 5-year median forward P/E multiple stands at 17.6x. However, since ORCL has undergone a transformation in the AI era, the company deserves a multiple re-rating to the upside in my opinion.

For FY25, the company registered an EPS growth of 8.4%, as mentioned earlier. This falls short of its long-term mean EPS growth of 10.64% but is above its trailing 5-year CAGR of 7.13%. Furthermore, if one compares the mean long-term EPS growth of ORCL and its peers, then ORCL, in my opinion, should be trading at a similar multiple to CRM.

In my last article on CRM, I had assumed a forward P/E of 32.1x for the company. As such, I have assumed the same multiple (32.1x) for ORCL, slightly higher than my previous estimate of 31.9x.

According to Seeking Alpha, ORCL currently trades at a forward PEG ratio of 2.95, well above both the sector median (1.79) and its 5-year average (1.91). The table below shows the projected FY26 EPS based on these different PEG ratios. The forward P/E multiple remains at 32.1x and the FY25 EPS remains at $6.03 for all three scenarios.

Using a discount factor of 8.44%, obtained form Alpha Spread, the present value of the projected FY26 EPS comes in at $6.41. At a forward P/E of 32.1x, the price target would then be $206. At the time of writing this article, shares have already crossed $200, which implies that there is limited upside. This cannot be surprising given that the stock would be up more than 20% YTD if it finishes around the $200 levels today. According to Seeking Alpha’s Momentum scale, the stock has decent momentum in the short-term, as reflected by a grade of B+. Taking things together, I am downgrading my rating on the stock from a Hold to a Buy.

Despite the downgrade, I continue to love the company. The performance of this company has been remarkable, and I don’t see any reason why the company would slow down. My rating downgrade is purely a valuation call. Should the stock see any pullback, then in my opinion, ORCL is one name that investors should prioritize adding to their portfolios.

Concluding Thoughts

ORCL finished the quarter strongly, beating on both the top- and bottom-lines, thereby reversing its recent trend of misses. The demand growth for OCI continues to remain strong and one of the key takeaways of this quarter is that this demand growth is getting translated into revenue growth.

The company’s investments are starting to pay off, as evidenced by the first quarter of double-digit revenue growth for the company and the projected FY26 revenue growth expected to double y/y. At the same time, the surging RPO number shows that there is no fatigue in demand and given that the company is not facing any issues in getting GPUs to build the data centers, the company should be able to cater to this demand without any major issues.

My only concern with respect to ORCL is the valuation. Post earnings release, the stocks has already surged nearly 13% and crossed the $200 mark. As such, there is limited upside at current levels, based on my assumptions.

Having said that, while I am downgrading my rating on the stock from a Buy to a Hold, this is purely a valuation call and is targeted towards investors who are yet to get in the name. I don’t plan to sell any of my shares yet as momentum in the stock has certainly improved. Given that the momentum in demand is also translating into revenue momentum, this improvement shouldn’t come as a surprise to investors.

data.title: FLC: Discounted Valuation Could Present An Opportunity
data.description:
Overview

Flaherty & Crumrine Total Return Fund (FLC) is a closed-end fund that focuses on providing investors with an attractive total return as its name implies. The fund aims to achieve these results through a diverse range of preferred securities and various debt-focused investments. The fund's strategy means that FLC will underperform more traditional equities or ETFs from a price growth perspective. So despite the name, a fund like FLC shouldn't be utilized by investors who seek to maximize the total return achieved within their portfolio.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, FLC's price has increased by about 9.2%. When including all distributions that were paid to shareholders over the same period, the total return jumps up above 17%. The fund offers a starting dividend yield of 6.8% and issues its distributions on a monthly basis. This structure makes the fund attractive for income investors who want to create a stream of supplemental dividend income that may be more resistant to the volatility of traditional market indexes like the S&P 500 or Nasdaq.

FLC has an inception dating back to 2003, which means that we have over two decades worth of operating history to reference. In relation to the fund's price history, FLC now trades at a sizeable discount to NAV valuation. This discount can be an indication that FLC's price level is attractive for share accumulation. However, this discount may also be a reflection of the underlying weakness of the higher interest rate environment. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that FLC implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

Based on the most updated fact sheet, FLC has total net assets of $191M that are spread across approximately 246 different holdings. The fund chooses to invest the majority of its assets into preferred securities, which are structured as hybrid securities that have characteristics of both bonds and stocks. These preferred investments are essentially a blend between equity and debt. Additionally, the fund provides investors with some global exposure to regions outside of the US. The breakdown by region is as follows:

  • United States: 70.03%.
  • France: 7.71%.
  • Canada: 7.64%.
  • United Kingdom: 5.7%.
  • Spain 4.55%.
  • Mexico: 1.28%.
  • Bermuda: 1.11%.
  • Ireland: 0.58%.
  • Switzerland: 0.54%.
  • Australia: 0.50%.

Looking at the breakdown by sector, we can see that FLC is most heavily concentrated on Bank investments, making up 55.12% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Insurance and Utility investments, accounting for 20.86% and 11.44% of assets respectively. However, since Bank investments account for such a large percentage of the fund's assets, we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on FLC's daily price change and total return potential.

Looking at the top ten issuers, we can see many recognizable large-cap firms here. For instance, Société Générale Société anonyme (OTCPK:SCGLF) is the largest holding at 4%, followed by varying allocations to peers like MetLife, Inc. (MET), Liberty Mutual Group, BNP Paribas SA (OTCQX:BNPQF), and Citigroup Inc. (C) just to name a few. Despite the diverse range of exposure to different high quality names, FLC is likely to underperform its own holdings due to the emphasis on income generation, meaning that FLC will essentially sacrifice its earnings in order to pay out distributions.

Lastly, investors need to consider the risks associated with the fund's use of leverage. FLC implements leverage equivalent to 38.2% of assets, in an effort to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. I consider this amount of leverage to be quite aggressive in this high interest rate environment and I tend to prefer the use of leverage to be kept below 20%. This is because leverage can drastically increase the risk profile for a fund and has the ability to amplify losses if allocated towards an investment that doesn't perform as anticipated.

Valuation

Since FLC operates as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its assets. Therefore, FLC currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 8.15%. Looking back over the last ten years below, we can see that the current discount level sits much lower than its usual range. For reference, FLC has traded at an average discount of about 9.4% over the last three years, due to the fund reaching a discount to NAV as high as 15% at the end of 2023. This larger discount window can present an opportunity for accumulation.

I reviewed the latest annual report for 2024 in an effort to get better clarity about the fund's internal health. The first thing that stands out is that net investment income levels have remained fairly consistent over the last few years, but there was a decline in 2024. Net investment income amounted to $1.04 per share while net realized gains landed at $2.27 per share. Net investment income has remained above $1.00 per share over the last five years, but there was a noticeable reduction in income since interest rates were raised throughout 2022 and 2023. The higher interest rate environment could be directly correlated to the lower earnings here, due to a higher cost of leverage or shrinking spreads of fixed rate preferred investments.

Therefore, the fund has become a lot more reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support the distribution payouts. The issue is that net realized gains are typically dependent on positive market conditions to drive growth. As you can imagine, it can be difficult for a fund to capture net realized gains when market indexes are declining. This is why we see moments where FLC suffers from large net realized losses, such as during 2022 when net realized losses were $4.91. As a result of this weakness, the fund's NAV growth has lagged. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $21.97 per share.
  • 2021: $22.18 per share.
  • 2022: $17.22 per share.
  • 2023: $16.32 per share.
  • 2024: $18.57 per share.

Thankfully, FLC has been able to regain some positive momentum from its lows of 2023. However, market uncertainty around the future of interest rate cuts, the impact of President Trump's tariffs, and shifts in consumer habits could present headwinds for the fund. I would like to see net investment income levels improve, but I believe that the fund would only see a meaningful improvement in performance when interest rates are reduced.

For reference, interest rates still remain near their decade highs, and we can see how FLC shares an inverse relationship. When interest rates were cut to near-zero levels in response to the pandemic, FLC's price rapidly increased. Conversely, FLC's price declined throughout 2022 and 2023 as rates were hiked. Therefore, future interest rate cuts can serve as a positive catalyst for growth. However, it seems like the Fed is choosing to leave rates unchanged as they continue to monitor the current macroeconomic environment.

Dividend Sustainability

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.0957 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 6.8%. If we multiply this monthly distribution amount by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.1484 per share. Looking back on the same annual report, FLC has been able to generate earnings exceeding this amount more often than not. Just for reference, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: $1.74 per share.
  • 2021: $1.74 per share.
  • 2022: ($3.52) per share.
  • 2023: $0.19 per share.
  • 2024: $3.31 per share.

As you can see, FLC has been able to out-earn its distribution amount in three of the last five years. Given the market's decline throughout 2022, underperformance here is understandable. Despite the solid coverage rate, the dividend history below reveals that FLC has a history of reducing its payouts over the last decade. We can see how the payouts have been on a downward trend, but I do think that this can be reversed once interest rates are reduced. The lowered dividend actually helps the fund better preserve its NAV health since a lower distribution can prevent the fund from issuing payouts that have a direct negative effect on its NAV.

Unfortunately, I believe there is a chance that distributions can be reduced if the interest rate environment is not improved in the near term. The possibility of a dividend cut makes it very difficult to recommend FLC as a reliable source of income. The whole point of maintaining exposure to a high-yield asset like FLC is the income that it can provide. If that income is threatened, it defeats the purpose of holding a position.

On a more positive note, the distributions have helped the fund maintain a strong total return since inception. In the chart below, we can see how FLC's price since inception has declined by nearly 33%. When including all distributions received, the total return sits above 304% since inception!

It's also worth mentioning that the distributions are funded by net investment income, which indicates that the dividends received would be classified as ordinary dividend income. Ordinary dividends are taxed at the highest rates, which means that a position in FLC may be best utilized in a tax-advantaged account.

Risk

Since FLC has exposure to debt securities, the biggest risk comes from the possibility of defaults. Thankfully, FLC has a large exposure to borrowers with credit ratings that are classified as investment grade, which helps mitigate these risks. However, the fund still has a sizeable allocation to debt securities with poor credit ratings. For reference, anything rated below a Baa on the Moody's scale is classified as below investment grade. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (A - Baa): 46.58%.
  • Below Investment Grade (Ba - Caa): 38.05%.
  • Not Rated: 15.37%.

Since the fund uses leverage, I believe these risks could be amplified if we remain in a higher for longer interest rate environment. Higher rates directly translate to a higher cost of debt for borrowers that have their loans structured on a floating rate basis. This can negatively impact a borrower's operating margins since a larger portion of earnings needs to be allocated towards interest expenses.

With the ongoing tariff situation, I believe that a rising cost of goods could ultimately lead to higher inflation and slower economic growth. In this scenario, interest rates are likely to remain elevated. S&P Global also has a pessimistic estimate that the default rate on leveraged loans could increase to 3% by December 2025. This estimate also assumes that there's an escalation in the trade policies. While FLC has a large allocation to highly-rated borrowers, there's still a chance that the fund's price trend can be impacted by the rest of the debt market.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain a hold rating on FLC for now due to the uncertainty around the interest rate environment and the possibility for the price to remain suppressed due to defaults. The fund utilizes an aggressive amount of leverage, which increases the overall risk profile of the fund. As interest rates remain high, the spread on preferred and debt investments seems to have caused net investment income levels to shrink in recent years. Despite the recent NAV growth, I would like to see improvement in the underlying performance and resilience against net realized losses. I think FLC is a solid fund, but this is not the ideal time for entry.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery Split: Repeating The Error Of The AT&T Deal Is No Solution
data.description:
Above: Warner's IP is strong but a long tail from origins.

Everyone makes mistakes, but if you make the same mistake twice it's no longer a mistake, it's a choice... - Unknown

Here's some selected fledgling rhetoric back in a 2018 cheer for the creation of the new Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) spin off from AT&T (T):

Our collective assets and diverse businesses model portfolio across film, TV and streaming... we are confident we can bring more choice to consumers than was had before. I can't wait for both teams to come together with a plan for great storytelling... - Source: Statements from WBD executives during the April 2018 announcement.

WBD stock was trading at $20.26 at the time. It's now around $11, nearly 50% down from the 2018 deal. The new 2025 deal is structured as a tax-free spinoff. But after nearly five years inside AT&T, up came the white flag – a spin-off.

Comments on the just announced WBD split into two companies: One fundamentally focused on production from the studio and streaming business like HBOMax. The second new company will be Global Networks, inheriting legacy TV with CNN, TNT Sports (post NBA), top free to air channels (Europe), and Bleacher Report. The WB studio and streaming CEO is thus far named as David Zaslav, and the Global Network will be under Gunnar Wiedenfels.

2025 Spin-Off

Zaslav: The division will provide enhanced strategic focus and flexibility and will better position storied brands across entertainment, sports, and news to drive shareholder value.

Further, he added:

By operating as two distinct and optimized companies in the future, we are empowering these iconic brands with the sharper focus and strategic flexibility they need to compete most effectively in the evolving media landscape.

Not to be in any way flippant about the wording used by WBD executives in announcing the deals seven years apart, I'm reminded of that old songbook classic, "It seems to me I've heard that song before..." This is no shade on the intentions of WBD. We know that such press release clichés abound. I've written more than a few myself. The final product is the result of a process that begins in the PR department, goes up the line to the executive level, is circulated around lawyers, finance analysts and finally the CEO and ultimately the board before it hits the Xerox machine. Their aim is clarity, truthfulness (as far as law allows), color for the rationales, statements from the CEO.

So that criteria has been met. That's why the seven-year spread between statements could be exchanged without much loss of understanding, either. The idea is to lead Mr. Market into some kind of buy in to the new deal that doesn't suggest too much self-interest to insiders.

The corporate WBD statements this time stress growing strength of brands, pursuit of enhanced investments in streaming, international, overall debt of $38b assumed by the Global unit aided by a $17.5b bridge loan arranged by JPMorgan to be refinanced before closing next year. In both WBD corporate statements, 2018 and 2025, we see strains of the same thesis of the spin-off. So what's different? What's happened in the seven years in between that imposed fundamental change?

Simply put: In 2018 WBD was the result of AT&T having no other viable choice but to load up all of what began as WB and Discovery onto investors. Along with that equity dump came a $57b IOU left for the heirs to wrestle. Today, mostly the same bitter choice faces WBD holders. Substantially lower debt, that's good, a bigger streaming membership, also good. But otherwise the same deck of cards more or less they were handed in 2018.

Battling massive inherited debt ($57b) from the AT&T deal in 2018 took up the central positive theme to shareholders as the price of the common kept falling. It was a good strategy for a while, demonstrating WBD's financial discipline. But aside from periodic tweaks by Zaslav that did produce solid subscriber gains, there wasn't much to celebrate. The success of Max has been indicative of Zaslav and Malone reversing the name of Max. It began as MaxHBO with the wrongful assumption that consumer affection for Max content was driving growth. Events proved otherwise. HBO remained the gold standard for consumer response, so management wisely switched back to HBO/Max as its streaming flagship brand.

Former associates of mine recently retired from WBD (including HBO) in the period between the AT&T spinoff and 2023 guessed a split might be coming:

...because Zaslav and Malone are out of ideas once they reach a leverage ratio that works for new investors seeing possibilities for the stock.

I did a survey of DCF calls and intrinsic value calls I thought reflected a clear view of the media business future. Merged to a consensus, I came up with $26. I shared this calculation with entertainment industry pros, and they agreed that WBD content was definitely undervalued. They believed a post-division price in the $20s was doable. Debt to FCF was 8.6X against an industry median of 0.1X. At five years, some industry analysts are looking for a base case price of $26-$29 for the Warner piece and are yet to feel confident about Global due to uncertainties surrounding the future of CNN and other weak sisters.

2024 WBD Annual Capsule Report

Revenue: $19.7b (3% yoy decline)

Net (loss) ($11,482b) yoy from ($3.1b)

Predominant overall operating strategy: Generate FCF from linear and divert it to pay down debt. WBD has done an impressive job repaying over $20b to date, and proposes to dump most of the remaining $37b+ on its proposed Global Network division.

2025 and Beyond

Our thesis here is this: If you believe that Warner and Global divided can change investor perceptions that are now obstacles to positive outlooks, that trigger upsides in trading. Some analysts have gathered around a PT of $12.50. The bet: The film release schedule contains some blockbuster hopefuls like Minecraft spread between 2025-2028. If at least four releases over the studio gross estimates, WBD theatrical is headed in the right direction. Superman will debut on July 11, within its potential holding the fate of the entire film schedule for the year.

If you also believe the $17.5b bridge loan by JPMorgan to be repaid by the close of the deal in 2026 as promised, you have the track record of WBD repaying a most impressive $20b.

Ratings of bonds now downgraded to junk is a contributor to bearish sentiment we expect to dog the shares in the months ahead.

The film will bet the stalking horse for the whole reimagined DC universe to come. If it goes down as just another reboot from what's now a distant past (the original comic book appeared in 1938, the first movie in 1977), then it's "Houston we have a problem" situation for the division.

If you think the man of steel will come to the rescue, taking a few other Warner films along the ride, at under $10 a share, it's a moderate buy. Strong grosses of its theatrical releases will water the shares.

Add to that the trend of growing streaming subs with 5.1m added in 1Q25 positioning WBD with a good chance to reach its goal of 150m subs within the next few years, and you have a basis for upside. Then consider both the disappearance of linear costs plus its revenue and the other losses shifted to Global's debt, and you do have a case for a ride over 20.

In brief, we have a classic "beauty is in the eye of the beholder" dilemma posed both to holders and would-be investors that the proposed division does not solve. It comes down to assets already held, plus the hope that the consolidation of creative juices will produce more content than it already has in the six-to-seven years of WBD under the current management.

So if, on the other hand, you cannot see the above scenario playing out by 2026, it's time to still take a pass on either new WBD stock.

On fundamentals, the breakup of WBD except for the financial moves to match up debt by bridge loans and fiscal activity echoes the same underlying problems of the first spin-off from AT&T: The best outcome for all might have been AT&T creating three new companies from the assets. One, The Warner Studio, two, the Streaming business, and three, selling off the entire linear portfolio and use the cash to reduce debt. The refocus and sharpening of the IP offerings talked about in both deals could have had the benefit of investment promised by the two new companies.

Conclusion

WBD might inch up a few dollars between now and the 2026 close, but it's difficult to see circumstances other than gigantic grosses for films like Superman, or a continuing dramatic growth in streaming to its 150m goal that will keep the ride going yet.

Right now, film grosses and streaming gains are the ticket to ride for holders to hang in until the year plays out. Or continue to sit out the stock until you see real growth coming from the new Warner this year, It's tough to make a bull case for either of the two. Below: A struggle to get to over $20 until 2027?

data.title: iRhythm: Promising Outlook - So Long As Past Mistakes Are Not Repeated
data.description:
Investment Overview

I've covered iRhythm Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ:IRTC) - a maker and provider of remote cardiac monitoring devices - twice before in notes for Seeking Alpha.

The first, from September 2021, advised readers to buy stock based on the likely reversal of a decision to slash reimbursement received by the company from Medicare by two-thirds. This call worked out very well as by March 202, shares had risen from ~$50 per share to >$160.

My next call in October last year was more cautious - the company's stock had sunk to lows of <$60 per share after the FDA had issued a warning letter to the company, relating to changes it had made to its Zio AT System, which the FDA felt required a 510(K) submission. iRhythm felt the changes it made did not require FDA approval, but the agency disagreed.

iRhythm stock had begun to soar after the FDA granted clearance for iRhythm's retrospective 510(K) submission for Zio AT, but I preached caution, writing that:

It is also worth noting that the FDA's warning letter apparently also accused iRhythm of failing to report patient deaths in a timely manner, labelling violations, and quality system violations.

As such, it seems that iRhythm is far from "out of the woods" in terms of the various investigations ongoing, requests for data, and various violations.

Perhaps I should have been more positive as, just like after my September 2021 call, the stock kept climbing, reaching its highest value since September 2022 this week - $147 per share, which translates to a market cap of $4.7bn.

Update On Issues With The FDA

Is iRhythm now "out of the woods"? Not necessarily.

In its Q1 2025 quarterly report/10-Q submission, the company notes it received a second 510(K) approval related to "design modifications and labeling updates" identified in the FDA's prior warning letter, but also notes:

Our receipt of 483 observations following July 2024 FDA inspections of our Cypress and San Francisco FDA-registered facilities centered on complaint handling and medical device reporting, risk analysis regarding the involvement of the technicians to prepare the Zio ECG reports, the corrective and preventive action process, process controls and statistical techniques.

We timely submitted our initial responses regarding the July 2024 483 observations to the FDA on August 21, 2024, and provided supplemental information on September 6, 2024. In these responses, we committed to a number of follow-up actions, and we intend to work with the FDA to resolve the issues identified.

FDA may determine that our remediation efforts to date or our responses to the 2024 483 observations are insufficient or unsatisfactory, or the FDA may decide that it does not agree with the plans and commitments we have outlined in our previous communications. FDA could issue another warning letter, issue a consent decree in collaboration with the DOJ, and/or require recall or cessation of marketing and shipping our Zio device.

This does not seem especially encouraging, although iRhythm's CEO Quentin Blackford told analysts on the Q1 2025 earnings call:

We are on time and on track with all of the remediation activities that we identified and committed to the agency, and we will continue to close those out. And I expect by the mid part of the year, we will have finished all of our remediation activities specific to the warning letter and specific to the 483s.

What I can't tell you is when the FDA might get back out here. I think with all the turnover within the agency, it's a little bit difficult to predict exactly what that might look like. And I think we're just going to need some time and clearly some direction from them on what they expect.

In summary, then, pending a successful re-assessment of its facilities, timeline unknown, iRhythm believes it is on course to resolve these issues - management ought to be able to handle this.

Blowout Q1 Wows Wall Street

iRhythm has grown its revenues from $36.1m in 2016, to $592m in 2024m, although the company has not made an operating profit in either of these years nor any in-between. Net income in the past four years, working backward, has been $(113m), $(123m), $(116m), and $(101m).

In Q1 2025 the company reported revenues of $158.7m - up >20% year-on-year - gross profit of $109m, but a net loss of $(30.7m), or $(0.97) per share, Cash position was reported as $520.6m, and guidance for 2025 projected to be $690 - $700m - up ~17.5% on 2024 at the midpoint. The company added that "adjusted EBITDA margin for the full year 2025 is expected to range from approximately 7.5% to 8.5% of revenues."

These results were reported on May 1st and triggered a surge in the company; share price, from $110 per share, to >$145 by late May. Clearly, Wall Street is more focused on growth than profitability at this time. Based on revenue guidance, the forward price-to-sales ratio for iRhythm is ~6.75x, which I would call relatively high, especially when we consider price to adjusted EBITDA would be >270x, by my calculation.

iRhythm says (in its Q1 earnings presentation) it is producing >2 million patient reports annually, but adds that there are >27m patients in the U.S. who may benefit from ambulatory cardiac monitoring, and a target market of >5m tests in Europe and APAC.

As we can see above, iRhythm sees itself as part of the AI "revolution," although, if the AI is being employed at iRhythm's Cypress and San Francisco FDA-registered facilities, we may be inclined to question its true present value, given the ongoing investigation.

Looking Ahead - Reasons To Be Cheerful, Or Fearful?

iRhythm's CEO told analysts on the Q1 earnings call:

Following record new account onboarding achieved during 2024, we were very pleased to observe significant volume demand throughout the first quarter of 2025 for both Zio Monitor and Zio AT. Demand within our Zio Monitor product line originated from cardiologists, electrophysiologists and primary care physicians, and we were encouraged to see another large national innovative health channel partner begin implementing the Zio Monitor for their population.

The CEO also underlined the value proposition as follows:

Based upon recent real-world retrospective claims analysis performed by our partner, EVERSANA, for every 1,000 patients with certain comorbid conditions who are diagnosed with an arrhythmia earlier in the care pathway, it may result in over $10 million in downstream cost avoidance by preventing events that could increase health care resource utilization, such as emergency department visits or hospitalizations.

The "primary care" channel is iRhythm's priority, and given the new HHS and FDA regimes have signaled that they are strongly in favor of AI, and expect to rely on it more and more, I would not be surprised to see the company make solid progress in that field under these new administrations.

Expansion in European markets such as Switzerland, Austria, the Netherlands and Spain, and a reimbursement win in Japan are also noted, with the latter apparently the "second-largest ambulatory cardiac monitoring market globally," presumably behind the U.S.

Finally, iRhythm expects to launch a mobile cardiac telemetry (“MCT”) monitoring product imminently, with the CEO commenting:

As an example, having 14 days of continuous wear on an MCT product is really unlike any other product that's out there today. You're wearing multiple patches even to get to 14 days with most competitors.

In summary, lots of positives in front of iRhythm, in my view. Wider adoption of its technology, improving technology, more and more data incoming, vital in terms of developing new and better AI, a potential move into the primary care channel, and a chance to settle outstanding issues with the FDA.

It is not hard to see why iRhythm stock has been rising, although the CEO himself has preached some caution, particularly in response to analysts' questions around whether, in light of the positive Q1, full-year guidance was not lifted higher?

In my experience, it is not uncommon for the market's expectations to run ahead of management's, and then, for those expectations to become demands, which, if not met, result in shares being sold off. Where iRhythm is concerned, however, I believe there is more upside to explore before the market loses patience, so long as revenues keep growing - a beat in Q2 would be met with enthusiasm, I suspect.

Concluding Thoughts - After A Strong Quarter, Am I Bullish Ahead Of Q2 Earnings

Back in 2022, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) paid $16.6bn to acquire Abiomed and its heart pump devices, to bolster its cardiac medical devices division - which continues to be one of its fastest growing divisions today.

iRhythm's products are not as sophisticated as Abiomed's which were very much in the primary care space, but nevertheless, the company's ambition is to grow into that space. I also note the $32bn market cap valuation for Dexcom, once again emphasizing that iRhythm is not yet in this space, although its MCT product feels like an exciting launch step in that direction.

iRhythm stock is up >60% year-to-date, but up only 24% on a five-year basis. The issue that is potentially holding me back from upgrading my last "Hold" rating to a "Buy" is twofold.

Firstly, on this occasion, I am covering iRhythm at a time when its share price is buoyant, as opposed to being weighed down by specific issues. Secondly, the company's Achilles heel appears to be the very thing that is supposed to be its strength - its ability to precisely monitor patients to the very highest of standards, and be relied upon by physicians, patients, and health insurers.

My slight fear is that the FDA does return to iRhythm's facilities and finds them unsatisfactory - in such an event, shares will crater once again, in response to a major setback just as they did in 2021, and in 2023.

Nevertheless, because I could also see iRhythm becoming an acquisition target, and because of its ability to leverage AI capabilities at a time when AI can still add billions to a market cap valuation, I am going to stick my neck out slightly and upgrade the stock to a "Buy."

data.title: Kering: Deep, Deep, In The Mud
data.description:
Kering SA's (OTCPK:PPRUF) stock is in a 78% drawdown since the 2021 peak, as the owner of Gucci and YSL is failing to reignite demand.

Kering’s nosedive started long before the rest of the industry began to see signs of demand faltering, and it’s still lagging far behind most of its competitors.

Is it now finally time to try and catch this falling knife? Let’s find out.

Introduction

The Pinault family has long been considered as Arnault's LVMH Moët Hennessy - Louis Vuitton, Société Européenne (OTCPK:LVMHF) the biggest rivals. The two conglomerates exercise a similar strategy, by acquiring brands and aggregating them under one house.

The goal is to create economies of scale, and synergies in marketing, and exploit the know-how across the organization to the benefit of each of the individual brands.

Of course, there’s also a major disadvantage to scale, at least theoretically, in an area where being new, authentic, unique, and a trend-setter, is key.

For quite some time, these conglomerates were able to overcome this challenge and were performing extraordinarily, with Kering actually leading the way.

Then, as LVMH continued racing ahead, Kering began to flop, culminating in the departure of one of Gucci’s most successful creative directors, Alessandro Michele.

As sales growth plummeted, the gap from peers widened, and Kering’s nosedive went in full swing.

Ever since, investors who tried to catch this falling knife were mistaken, and have seen meaningful losses.

In my three years of covering the stock, I repeatedly said that the right move is to wait for an actual sign of a turnaround, as the risk of mistiming the buy outweighs the possible upside from being early.

So far, this line of thought turned out to be correct. Is something different now?

The Only Positive In Kering’s Results Is That The Growth Gap Seemingly Found A Bottom

When investors talked about closing the gap from peers, what they probably envisioned was Kering climbing, rather than peers coming down. And yet, the latter is what has occurred.

In the first quarter of 2025, Kering revenues were €3.9 billion, down 14% from last year. On a segment basis, Gucci dropped 24%, Saint Laurent was down 8%, Other Houses dropped 11%, and Bottega Veneta and Eyewear rose 4% each.

Geographically, all regions saw notable declines. Asia Pacific was down 25%, North America and Western Europe declined by 13%, Japan was down 11%, and the rest of the world was up 1%. On a channel basis, retail dropped 16% and wholesale was down 9%, a bad reflection on the appeal of Kering’s brands, which are seemingly doing better with wholesalers.

Kering’s results were again among the worst in its peer group, with Hermès International Société en commandite par actions (OTCPK:HESAF) and Compagnie Financière Richemont SA (OTCPK:CFRHF) growing much faster, and LVMH declining at a much smaller rate.

So, unfortunately, there’s still no sign whatsoever of a turnaround. And, logically, why would there be?

Still Waiting For A Real Internal Shakeup

Turnarounds don’t happen out of the blue. To get out of the mud, most companies need to implement painful yet necessary changes.

In my view, one of the biggest reasons that major changes haven’t been made at Kering yet is that the Pinault family, as the controlling shareholders, aren’t that pressured. I mean, they are already extremely rich, they love what they do, and I’m not sure if they care that much about the stock market or how much growth they generate relative to peers.

Let me rephrase, it’s not that they don’t care, they are just not pressured into caring, like a hired CEO would have been.

Still, there are some changes that are worth talking about.

First, Francesca Bellettini, previous CEO of Saint Laurent, was moved to the role of Deputy CEO in charge of brand development for the entire group. Saint Laurent hasn't over-achieved for the group for quite some time and Belletini might be able to help the rest of the brands, specifically Gucci.

Second, Cedric Charbit, the previous CEO of Balenciaga, was moved to replace Francesca in her previous role. To me, this isn’t a great move on paper, as Balenciaga was one of the worst-performing brands for Kering under Charbit’s leadership, and I’m not sure how putting him in charge of the second-largest brand in the group would yield better results.

Third, on the creative side, Demna Gvasalia was appointed as creative director at Gucci, moving from the role at Balenciaga.

All these moves might appear to be meaningful, but I’m skeptical. This is not the earthquake that, I think, is required to truly change things here.

Valuation & Prospects

Right now, I see no reason to expect Kering will deliver results that are anywhere near its peers. And, as peers themselves are struggling, this means Kering’s results will be far from inspiring.

With those depressed prospects in mind, Kering’s valuation does little to make the setup any easier:

At €175 per Paris-listed share, and $204 per ADR, Kering trades at 19 times earnings. This is not that far off LVMH, which is doing better. It's also not that far off from Richemont's adjusted multiple, which is doing much better.

So, to me, this valuation isn’t attractive.

Conclusion

Kering’s struggles continue, and there’s still no sign of a turnaround. The company’s moves to change things remain quite underwhelming in my view, with a few internal mixes and matches, and not much more than that.

Kering’s valuation doesn’t reflect the crisis I believe it’s in, which means I continue to encourage investors to ‘Sell’.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
